event_name: Disney Princess Summer Movies: Beauty and the Beast (1991)
venue: Earl and Rachel Smith Strand Theatre, Marietta
blurb: The 1991 Beauty and the Beast is one of those films that hits differently in a proper old theater, and the Strand is exactly that kind of venue. The July 15 showing adds a princess meet-and-greet before the film for kids who want the full experience, doors open an hour ahead for photos. For the 2:30pm showing specifically, there's also a live pre-show concert on the Mighty Allen Theatre Organ thirty minutes before the film starts, which is a genuinely cool detail that most movie screenings can't touch. This is a summer Wednesday afternoon well spent, especially if you've got young kids in tow or you're just a person who knows that "Be Our Guest" absolutely slaps and has no shame about it.
scoring_notes: The young families segment of the Perimeter audience is a natural fit here. The Strand Theatre venue adds a layer of charm and legitimacy beyond a typical multiplex. The organ pre-show is a genuinely unique detail. Scores slightly lower than the adult-skewing events because the Perimeter newsletter skews more adult than East Cobb, but the young families presence still makes this worth including.

event_name: Music on the Hill: The M-80s
blurb: Roswell City Hall's lawn turns into a full-on 80s throwback party on July 10 when The M-80s take the stage for Music on the Hill. The drill is simple: spread out a blanket, unpack a picnic, open something cold, and let a band that clearly loves this decade do its job. Big hair anthems, loud guitars, the kind of songs you know every word to even though you'd have a hard time explaining how. This is part of Roswell's America250 summer series, so the energy around it is a little elevated compared to a typical free concert. It's also a Friday night, which makes it an easy first-stop for the evening before deciding where the rest of the night goes.
scoring_notes: Free outdoor concert with a clear 80s nostalgia hook plays well for the 38-median-age Perimeter audience, who grew up in or around that era. The Roswell location is accessible from the Perimeter. Not the most unique event on the list, but the Friday night timing and the picnic-on-the-lawn format make it feel like a solid evening out rather than a detour.

event_name: The Beach Boys at Cobb Energy
venue: Cobb Energy Performing Arts Centre, Atlanta
blurb: The Beach Boys have been on the road longer than most of their fans have been alive, and in 2026 they're marking the 60th anniversary of Pet Sounds while also celebrating America's 250th. That combination, at Cobb Energy, makes this one worth circling on the calendar. Pet Sounds is widely considered one of the greatest albums ever recorded. "Wouldn't It Be Nice," "God Only Knows," "Sloop John B." You already know the songs. Hearing that catalog live in a real performing arts venue is a different experience than the outdoor festival version. The Perimeter audience skews toward the 35-50 range where nostalgia for this music is real, whether you grew up with it yourself or inherited it from a parent who played it on every road trip. Grab tickets before this one fills up.
scoring_notes: The 60th anniversary of Pet Sounds is a genuine hook that elevates this above a typical touring concert. The Cobb Energy venue is close and familiar to the Perimeter audience. Median age of 38 means the audience has a reasonable connection to this music, though it skews slightly older. Still a strong pick because of the milestone angle and the venue quality.

blurb: Seven Studio Ghibli films on the big screen, in luxury recliners, over what looks like a multi-day run at Aurora Cineplex in Roswell. If you've never seen Spirited Away or My Neighbor Totoro on a proper theater screen, this is the window. Ghibli films have a visual quality that genuinely looks different at scale, the kind of hand-drawn detail that gets lost on a 55-inch TV. The July 11 screening block runs 5pm to 7pm, which makes it an easy early-evening plan before dinner. The Perimeter crowd has a strong young professional and young family presence, and both groups have Ghibli fans in them. Nostalgia for the adults, discovery for the kids. That's a hard combo to beat.
scoring_notes: Studio Ghibli has broad cross-demographic appeal in an educated, urban-adjacent audience. The luxury recliners angle at Aurora Cineplex makes this feel like a treat rather than just a movie. The 5-7pm window is practical for families and adults alike. Ghibli on the big screen is genuinely rare and worth highlighting.

event_name: Scene on the Green: The Tempest
venue: The Art Place at Mountain View, Marietta
blurb: Free outdoor Shakespeare on a summer night is one of those things that sounds like a lot until you actually show up and realize it's just a great evening. Cobb County Parks is putting on The Tempest at The Art Place at Mountain View, and the setup is exactly what you want: bring your blanket, find a spot on the lawn, and watch magic, romance, and a shipwreck play out under the stars. The Tempest is one of Shakespeare's best for an outdoor setting too. It's a story literally set on an island with storms and sorcery and a cast of characters you don't need a lit degree to enjoy. Three and a half hours of runtime means you're not rushing home. Pack a cooler, get there a little early to settle in, and let the night do its thing.
scoring_notes: An educated adult audience (70% bachelor's degree or higher) is the natural home for free outdoor Shakespeare. The adult-skewing Perimeter demographic fits this better than a family-heavy audience would. Free admission removes any price friction entirely. The Tempest is a strong selection for outdoor performance. Good uniqueness score because live outdoor theater is genuinely less common than a concert or movie night.

blurb: It's the 4th of July and Dunwoody is doing it the right way. The parade steps off at 9am from the intersection of Mount Vernon Road and Jett Ferry Road, winds 2.7 miles west down Mt. Vernon, through Dunwoody Village, and wraps up with a festival area on the Walgreens side of Dunwoody Village Pkwy. The whole route takes about an hour to walk, so you've got time to grab a coffee first. This one hits different when it's literally in your backyard. No highway, no parking nightmare, no traveling 45 minutes for a fireworks show you've seen a hundred times. Just your neighborhood, a parade, and an excuse to be outside on a holiday morning before the heat fully kicks in. Bring the kids, bring the dog, bring whoever is visiting from out of town.
scoring_notes: The Perimeter audience overlaps heavily with Dunwoody. This is literally a neighborhood event, which gives it a local relevance score other candidates can't match. The mixed family skew of the audience (young families AND young professionals) means a 4th of July parade plays well across the board. It's a recurring annual event, but the 4th of July itself only comes once a year, so the uniqueness dip is minor.

blurb: You've been to Braves games. You've walked the Battery. But have you ever stood in the dugout after dark with a beer in your hand, looking up at the stars from the same spot where the roster runs out before a first pitch? That's the pitch for this one. Moonlight Tours at Truist Park put you behind the scenes of one of baseball's newest ballparks with a guide, a press box stop, and a cold Blue Moon waiting for you at the Blue Moon Brewery where the tour kicks off. The brewery piece isn't a gimmick either. You get an actual walkthrough of the brewing process from a Molson Coors insider before the ballpark portion starts. The event description says it only comes around "once in a Blue Moon" and look, they're not wrong. This is a genuinely different way to experience a venue you've probably driven past a hundred times. Must be 21+ to drink, but non-alcoholic options are available.
scoring_notes: Adult-skewing audience with disposable income and proximity to Truist Park makes this a natural fit. The behind-the-scenes ballpark angle is genuinely unique. Not a game night, not a concert, not a Battery restaurant visit. It's a totally different experience at a venue the Perimeter audience already has a relationship with. Strong uniqueness score.

event_name: Mezcal Pairing Dinner at Maya Speaktiki
blurb: This is not your standard dinner out. Maya Speaktiki is teaming up with Saca Espantos Mezcal Artesanal for a four-course pairing dinner built around one of the most interesting spirits in the game right now. Each course gets matched to a different expression of the mezcal, which means by the end of the night you've basically taken a guided tour through what artesanal mezcal actually tastes like when it's paired with food that was designed around it. It's the kind of thing that sounds fancy but feels genuinely fun, especially if you've always been curious about mezcal but never had someone walk you through it properly. One night, one restaurant, one mezcal brand. After this one's gone, it's gone.
scoring_notes: Perimeter audience skews adult, educated, and has the income to treat a ticketed dinner as a fun night out rather than a budget decision. This is a one-night-only event at a local Dunwoody venue, which makes it hyper-relevant geographically. The mezcal angle is specific and interesting enough to cut through the noise of generic 'dinner events.' Strong fit across all four factors.

venue: The Art Place at Mountain View, Marietta
blurb:
If you've got younger kids still in the house, or grandkids coming through this summer, put this one on the calendar.

Cobb County Parks is showing Zootopia 2 outdoors at The Art Place at Mountain View on July 11, part of their Scene on the Green series. Free admission, open lawn, bring a blanket or chairs, show up before dark. It's the simplest possible Saturday night with kids, and sometimes that's exactly what you need.

The Art Place is right at 3330 Sandy Plains Road in Marietta, which means it's about as local as it gets for East Cobb families. No tickets, no reservations, just show up.
scoring_notes: East Cobb skews toward teens and older kids, which limits the ceiling here. That said, the newsletter serves a mix of family types and grandparents with visiting grandkids are a real segment. Included as the most family-accessible option on the list, though it's the weakest of the eight on demographic fit. Free, hyperlocal, and zero friction keeps it relevant.

event_name: Joe Gransden's Sinatra: An American Icon
blurb:
Frank Sinatra never really goes out of style. The phrasing, the swing, the sheer confidence of the thing. Joe Gransden gets that, and he brings a full big band to back it up.

Gransden is one of Atlanta's most respected jazz musicians, and this tribute isn't a lounge act doing My Way at a wedding. It's a proper big band concert at the Roswell Cultural Arts Center, the kind of Sunday afternoon that feels like an occasion. Sinatra's American Icon catalog spans decades of American music history, and with the America 250 moment still in the air, the timing fits.

For East Cobb's empty nester crowd especially, a Sunday afternoon big band show in Roswell is about as good as a local weekend gets. Tickets and details at visitroswellga.com.
scoring_notes: Strong fit for East Cobb's empty nester segment and the higher-income, culturally engaged portion of the audience. Roswell is a short drive. The big band format and afternoon timing give it a more elevated feel than a typical weekend show. Slightly lower audience match because it skews older even within the East Cobb demographic, but that's not a disqualifier.

event_name: We Are the Champions: The Ultimate Queen Tribute Band
blurb:
If you've ever turned up Bohemian Rhapsody in the car and just let it go, this night is for you.

We Are the Champions brings the full Queen experience to the Jennie T. Anderson Theatre on July 11, an indoor venue that actually lets the sound hit the way it's supposed to. These aren't four guys going through the motions. Queen's catalog is one of the most demanding in rock history, and a tribute act that earns the name has to deliver on the big moments: the harmonies, the piano, the theatrical energy Freddie made famous.

It's a Saturday night, it's close, and the ticket price is nothing for a night out. Grab dinner on Marietta Square first and make a full evening of it.
scoring_notes: Queen's fanbase maps almost perfectly to East Cobb's 42-median-age audience. Indoor venue at the Anderson Theatre gives it a real concert feel. Works great for couples and empty nesters. The July 4th weekend is behind them by this point, making it a good follow-up night out mid-month.

blurb:
You don't have to drive to Texas for a real rodeo. Cobb County's got one.

The Cobb County Pro Rodeo runs through the full slate: bareback riding, calf roping, saddle-bronc riding, steer wrestling, team roping, barrel racing, and bull riding as the finale. This is professional-level competition, not a petting zoo. The athletes are real, the stakes are real, and bull riding as a closer is about as much live spectacle as you'll find in metro Atlanta on a Friday night.

For families with teens who've never been to a rodeo, this is a genuinely different night out. Something outside the usual rotation of concerts and restaurants. Check travelcobb.org for times and tickets before you head out.
scoring_notes: Uniqueness is the main draw here. A professional rodeo in Cobb County is legitimately rare and doesn't come through often. Strong appeal for families with teens who want something experiential and different. East Cobb's mix of established families makes this a solid pick for the 'we need something to do this weekend' crowd.

event_name: Music on the Hill: Piano Man vs. Rocket Man
blurb:
Billy Joel versus Elton John. On the lawn. For free. On a Friday night.

Music on the Hill brings out one of the more entertaining tribute matchups of the summer on July 10, pitting two of the most beloved piano men in rock history against each other in a single show. The lawn at Roswell City Hall is made for this: bring a blanket, unpack a picnic, open something cold, and let the music do the rest.

Both artists have deep catalogs with songs that East Cobb audiences know by heart, and a head-to-head tribute format keeps it fresh rather than just running through the hits in order. It's a low-effort, high-reward Friday. Roswell is right down the road, parking is easy, and you won't spend a dime to get in.
scoring_notes: Billy Joel and Elton John are almost perfectly matched to East Cobb's median age of 42. Both artists peak in cultural relevance for people who were 15–30 during their commercial prime. The free, outdoor, Friday-night format is frictionless for this audience. Recurring series lowers the uniqueness score, but the specific head-to-head tribute format is a differentiator.

blurb:
This is not your standard Braves game experience, and that's exactly the point.

Bats & Bites gives you early access to Truist Park before first pitch, starting with a guided tour that ends in the Outfield Market. Along the way you'll meet a former Braves player, sample new featured food items, and try a signature beer or cocktail. Then you head out to watch batting practice from the outfield, where catching a ball from a real BP session is genuinely on the table.

For Braves fans who've been to dozens of games and think they've seen it all, this is the version you haven't done yet. It's a couple hours of actual behind-the-scenes access at one of the best ballparks in baseball, right in your neighborhood. Details and tickets are at batteryatl.com.
scoring_notes: East Cobb's proximity to Truist Park makes this a near-backyard event. The experience goes well beyond a regular game day, which appeals to the established, higher-income audience that has already done the usual. The behind-the-scenes access and former player meet is a genuine differentiator. Strong fit for couples and empty nesters looking for something a little different.

event_name: Independence Eve Concert featuring Drivin N Cryin
venue: Glover Park at Marietta Square, Marietta
blurb:
Before the fireworks, there's Friday night on the Square.

Marietta is kicking off the Fourth of July weekend with a free outdoor concert headlined by Drivin N Cryin, one of Atlanta's own. These guys have been playing Southern rock since 1985, built their name at the 688 Club, earned a gold record, and are still going strong more than four decades later. This isn't a cover band or a random opener. It's a legitimately iconic Atlanta act playing a free show two minutes from East Cobb.

Bring a blanket, bring a lawn chair, grab food from the Square, and make a night of it. With the July 4th weekend in full swing and the America 250 celebration as the backdrop, the energy is going to be something else. Friday night, no tickets, no hassle. Just show up.
scoring_notes: East Cobb's median age of 42 means a solid chunk of this audience grew up with Drivin N Cryin on Atlanta radio. Southern rock with local roots, free, walkable from East Cobb, on a holiday Friday night. Hard to beat the combination. The July 4th weekend context adds even more energy.

event_name: City of Marietta's 4th in the Park Celebration
blurb:
This one has been a Marietta tradition for years, but 2026 is different. This is America's 250th birthday, and Marietta Square is throwing the kind of all-day celebration that matches the moment.

It kicks off with a morning parade featuring local school bands, military units, and civic organizations, runs through an afternoon of arts, crafts, food, and carnival games, and closes with a concert from Departure (one of the best Journey tribute acts around) and a full fireworks finale. That's 12 hours of something to do, right in your backyard.

If you've got family coming into town for the holiday, this is your answer for what to do with the whole crew. Blankets on the grass, food in hand, fireworks overhead. Free all day. Just show up.
scoring_notes: The America 250 angle makes a recurring event meaningfully special this year. East Cobb families, teens, and empty nesters with visiting family all have a reason to go. Free price point removes any friction. The 12-hour footprint means there's something for everyone regardless of age.

blurb: If there's one Fourth of July tradition in Cobb County that actually earns the word 'tradition,' it's this one. Marietta's Fourth in the Park has everything the day is supposed to feel like: a morning parade down the Square, arts and crafts vendors, food, carnival games, and then a full evening concert from Departure (the Journey tribute band that never disappoints) capped off with fireworks. It's the kind of day where you show up in the morning and somehow it's 10pm. Whether you're bringing the kids, meeting up with neighbors, or just want somewhere to be on the 4th that doesn't feel like a parking lot, Marietta Square delivers. No tickets, no hassle. Just show up.
scoring_notes: Annual anchor event for the area. East Cobb residents are close to Marietta Square and this is the definitive local July 4th celebration. Hits both established families and empty nesters. Free price point removes any friction. Uniqueness score docked slightly because it is an annual recurring event, but the scale and production make it a genuine can't-miss.
